paper_title: Structural Transformation and Competitiveness in Pakistan
research_answer: Beyond the immediate low-hanging fruit, Pakistan's product space analysis identifies a set of 'strategic bet' sectors that are further along the efficient frontier — requiring more of a capability leap but offering significantly higher sophistication and strategic value. Key examples include paint and coating manufacturing (strategic value ~$12,595), plastics packaging materials, film and sheet (~$12,453), and tire manufacturing (~$11,151). These sectors are more sophisticated than Pakistan's current exports and better connected to the industrial core of the product space, meaning success in them would open up a wider range of subsequent diversification options. They represent a middle path between the modest nearby opportunities (dairy, jewelry, oilseeds) and the very distant Vision 2030 targets (machinery, electronics). For comparison, even Pakistan's nearby 'low-hanging fruit' like dairy products offer high sophistication scores but are in world markets of $48 billion, while jewelry represents a $174 billion global market — but the strategic bets in intermediates like plastics and paints offer the additional advantage of better connecting Pakistan to higher-complexity manufacturing.
supporting_quotes: Table 3 Further along the Efficient Frontier: Strategic Bets... Paint and coating manufacturing... Plastics packaging materials, film and sheet... Tire manufacturing...

In terms of the market size of the opportunities, the largest are aluminum, dairy, jewelry, oilseeds, and coffee/tea production. Dairy products in particular offer high sophistication and strategic value.
